symptom: a shoulder that sat too high and could not lift the arm fully since birth
hook: one shoulder blade was parked up near the neck instead of lying flat on the back
whatYouSee: One scapula sits abnormally high and is smaller and rotated. It is occasionally bridged to the cervical spine by an extra bone.
whyItMatters: It is the most common congenital shoulder anomaly and limits shoulder abduction; it is frequently associated with Klippel-Feil and other spine anomalies.
treatment: Mild cases are observed. Significant functional or cosmetic deformity is corrected surgically and is best done in early childhood.
takeaway: A shoulder blade riding high near the neck from birth is the Sprengel deformity.

condition.keyFindings: a small, abnormally elevated and medially rotated scapula, sometimes connected to the cervical spine by an omovertebral bone

condition.takeaway: A shoulder blade riding high near the neck from birth is the Sprengel deformity.
condition.igTitle: THE SHOULDER THAT WOULD NOT DROP

generated.threadsCaption:
A patient came in with a shoulder that sat too high and could not lift the arm fully since birth.

Then the X-ray loaded 😭

And one shoulder blade was parked up near the neck instead of lying flat on the back.

Quick diagnosis challenge 🩻

What's the most likely diagnosis?

Wild guesses are welcome 👀
generated.threadsAnswer:
Answer: Sprengel deformity

👀 What you see:
One scapula sits abnormally high and is smaller and rotated. It is occasionally bridged to the cervical spine by an extra bone.

🦴 Why it matters:
It is the most common congenital shoulder anomaly and limits shoulder abduction; it is frequently associated with Klippel-Feil and other spine anomalies.

📝 Takeaway:
A shoulder blade riding high near the neck from birth is the Sprengel deformity.
